```feature
Feature: Bootstrap CLI surface — bd-init side-effect closure (lead-k8v round 2)

  @scenario_hash:32d99f6d4a2dad37 @bc:shopsystem-templates
  Scenario Outline: bootstrap invokes "bd init" with the "--skip-agents" flag so bd's own AGENTS.md and Claude settings generation is suppressed and the shop's canonical agent surface is the sole source of agent-prompt content
    Given an existing git repository at a target directory "<target>" with no ".beads/" directory
    When I invoke the "shop-templates" bootstrap entry point with shop type "<shop_type>", shop name "<shop_name>", and target directory "<target>"
    Then the exit code is 0
    And the target directory contains a ".beads/" directory
    And during the invocation a subprocess named "bd" was executed with first argument "init"
    And the argument list passed to that "bd" subprocess contains the exact token "--skip-agents"

    Examples:
      | shop_type | shop_name               | target                       |
      | bc        | shopsystem-messaging    | /tmp/example-bc-shop         |
      | lead      | shopsystem-product      | /tmp/example-lead-shop       |

  @scenario_hash:7d64bb8ed5a3f656 @bc:shopsystem-templates
  Scenario Outline: bootstrap leaves no top-level "AGENTS.md" and no ".claude/settings.json" in the target directory, regardless of shop type, because the shop's canonical agent surface is ".claude/agents/*.md" alone
    Given an existing git repository at a target directory "<target>" containing no top-level "AGENTS.md" and no file at ".claude/settings.json"
    When I invoke the "shop-templates" bootstrap entry point with shop type "<shop_type>", shop name "<shop_name>", and target directory "<target>"
    Then the exit code is 0
    And the target directory contains no top-level file named "AGENTS.md"
    And the target directory contains no file at the path ".claude/settings.json"

    Examples:
      | shop_type | shop_name               | target                       |
      | bc        | shopsystem-messaging    | /tmp/example-bc-shop         |
      | lead      | shopsystem-product      | /tmp/example-lead-shop       |

  @scenario_hash:5ec07c275350ba81 @bc:shopsystem-templates
  Scenario Outline: the canonical "CLAUDE.md" primer template for each shop type references the bd (beads) discipline that the shop is expected to follow, naming "bd prime" as the entry point a reader runs to learn the workflow
    When I ask the "shop-templates" package for the canonical "CLAUDE.md" primer template for shop type "<shop_type>" through its public template-access surface
    Then a non-empty template body is returned
    And the returned body contains the literal substring "bd prime"
    And the returned body contains at least one instruction directing the reader to run a "bd" subcommand as part of the shop's working discipline

    Examples:
      | shop_type |
      | bc        |
      | lead      |

  @scenario_hash:2afb5cba3ea3de25 @bc:shopsystem-templates
  Scenario Outline: the top-level "CLAUDE.md" that bootstrap writes into the target directory references the bd (beads) discipline by naming "bd prime", for both shop types, so the bootstrapped shop is self-describing about its working discipline without depending on bd's own AGENTS.md generation
    Given an existing git repository at a target directory "<target>" with no top-level "CLAUDE.md"
    When I invoke the "shop-templates" bootstrap entry point with shop type "<shop_type>", shop name "<shop_name>", and target directory "<target>"
    Then the exit code is 0
    And the target directory contains a top-level file named "CLAUDE.md"
    And the content of that file contains the literal substring "bd prime"

    Examples:
      | shop_type | shop_name               | target                       |
      | bc        | shopsystem-messaging    | /tmp/example-bc-shop         |
      | lead      | shopsystem-product      | /tmp/example-lead-shop       |
```

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-13 15:13:25Z)**_

Note: scenario 7d64bb requires the bootstrap produces no AGENTS.md and no .claude/settings.json. Currently bootstrap creates `.claude/agents/` (via `agents_dir.mkdir`) and writes `.claude/agents/<role>.md` files, but never creates `.claude/settings.json` itself, and never writes AGENTS.md. With `--skip-agents` on bd, neither will bd. So the existing bootstrap code is already compliant once the bd flag is added — scenario 7d64bb passes via the combination.

That works here because the preceding step asserts `contains a top-level file named "CLAUDE.md"`, but the step hardcodes the filename — if a future scenario wanted to assert substring content of `.gitignore` via "that file", the step would silently read CLAUDE.md instead. That's pre-existing tech debt unrelated to this dispatch.

<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Check bd init flags

`bd init --help 2>&1 | head -40`

```text
Initialize bd in the current directory by creating a .beads/ directory
and Dolt database. Optionally specify a custom issue prefix.

Dolt is the default (and only supported) storage backend. The legacy SQLite
backend has been removed. Use --backend=sqlite to see migration instructions.

Use --database to specify an existing server database name, overriding the
default prefix-based naming. This is useful when an external tool (e.g. an orchestrator)
has already created the database.

With --stealth: configures per-repository git settings for invisible beads usage:
  • .git/info/exclude to prevent beads files from being committed
  Perfect for personal use without affecting repo collaborators.
  To set up a specific AI tool, run: bd setup <claude|cursor|aider|...> --stealth

By default, beads uses an embedded Dolt engine (no external server needed).
Pass --server to use an external dolt sql-server instead. In server mode,
set connection details with --server-host, --server-port, and --server-user.
Password should be set via BEADS_DOLT_PASSWORD environment variable.

Auto-export is enabled by default. After every write command, bd exports
issues to .beads/issues.jsonl (throttled to once per 60s). This keeps
viewers (bv) and git-based workflows up to date without extra steps.
To disable: bd config set export.auto false

Non-interactive mode (--non-interactive or BD_NON_INTERACTIVE=1):
  Skips all interactive prompts, using sensible defaults:
  • Role defaults to "maintainer" (override with --role)
  • Fork exclude auto-configured when fork detected
  • Auto-export left at default (enabled)
  • --contributor and --team flags are rejected (wizards require interaction)
  Also auto-detected when stdin is not a terminal or CI=true is set.

Usage:
  bd init [flags]

Flags:
      --agents-file string       Custom filename for agent instructions (default: AGENTS.md)
      --agents-profile string    AGENTS.md profile: 'minimal' (default, pointer to bd prime) or 'full' (complete command reference)
      --agents-template string   Path to custom AGENTS.md template (overrides embedded default)
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Diff cli.py + conftest.py changes

`git diff src/shop_templates/cli.py tests/conftest.py 2>&1 | head -300`

```text
diff --git a/src/shop_templates/cli.py b/src/shop_templates/cli.py
index 8745e2f..4611d12 100644
--- a/src/shop_templates/cli.py
+++ b/src/shop_templates/cli.py
@@ -2,30 +2,80 @@
 
 Subcommands:
     list
-        Print available template names (one per line) to stdout.
+        Print available role-template names (one per line) to stdout.
     show <name>
         Print the named template's content to stdout. Exits non-zero
         with a stderr message when no template matches that name.
+    bootstrap --shop-type {bc,lead} --shop-name NAME --target DIR
+        Scaffold a new (existing-but-empty) shop repository at DIR:
+        write the canonical role-prompt copies for the shop type under
+        .claude/agents/, write a CLAUDE.md primer for the shop type,
+        write a top-level .gitignore, and invoke `bd init` as a
+        subprocess to initialize .beads/.
+    update --target DIR --shop-type {bc,lead}
+        Re-pour the bootstrap-managed agent files in DIR/.claude/agents/
+        from the current canonical package data. Reconcile against the
+        canonical role set for the shop type — add canonical files that
+        are missing, remove managed files whose canonical template no
+        longer exists. Does NOT touch CLAUDE.md, .gitignore, or .beads/.
 
-Templates live in the package as `templates/<name>.md` data files. The
-CLI is the public boundary; dispatchers, scaffold scripts, and any
-other consumer reads templates via this surface rather than by path.
+Role-prompt templates live in the package as `templates/<name>.md` data
+files. CLAUDE.md primer templates live as `templates/claude/<shop_type>.md`.
+The .gitignore template lives as `templates/gitignore.template`. The CLI
+is the public boundary; dispatchers, scaffold scripts, and any other
+consumer reads templates via this surface rather than by path.
 """
 from __future__ import annotations
 
 import argparse
+import subprocess
 import sys
 from importlib.resources import files
+from pathlib import Path
 
 
 _TEMPLATES_PKG = "shop_templates.templates"
+_CLAUDE_TEMPLATES_PKG = "shop_templates.templates.claude"
+
+# The canonical role-set assignment per shop type. The bootstrap surface
+# uses this to decide which role files to pour into .claude/agents/ for
+# a freshly-bootstrapped shop, and the update surface uses it to
+# reconcile an existing shop's .claude/agents/ against the current
+# canonical set.
+_CANONICAL_ROLE_SETS: dict[str, tuple[str, ...]] = {
+    "bc": ("bc-implementer", "bc-reviewer"),
+    "lead": ("lead-po", "lead-architect"),
+}
+
+_SHOP_TYPES = tuple(_CANONICAL_ROLE_SETS.keys())
+
+
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
+# Public template-access surface (importable; not just CLI-internal).
+#
+# Per scenario 0cce58eb573d3c91, the CLAUDE.md primer template must be
+# accessible "through its public template-access surface" and must NOT
+# be read from any path under the product's top-level working directory
+# at lookup time — i.e., it must be served from importlib.resources
+# package data, the same boundary the role-prompt templates use.
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
 
 
 def _list_template_names() -> list[str]:
-    """Return the sorted list of available template names (no extension)."""
+    """Return the sorted list of available role-template names.
+
+    Walks only the top-level package directory; sub-directories (e.g.
+    `templates/claude/`) are skipped. This keeps `shop-templates list`
+    output stable as new categories of package data (CLAUDE primers,
+    gitignore template) are added.
+    """
     pkg_root = files(_TEMPLATES_PKG)
     names = []
     for resource in pkg_root.iterdir():
+        # `is_file()` plus `.md` suffix isolates role-prompt templates
+        # from sub-directories like `templates/claude/`.
+        if not resource.is_file():
+            continue
         name = resource.name
         if name.endswith(".md"):
             names.append(name[: -len(".md")])
@@ -33,13 +83,56 @@ def _list_template_names() -> list[str]:
 
 
 def _read_template(name: str) -> str | None:
-    """Return the named template's content, or None if no such template."""
+    """Return the named role-template's content, or None if no such template."""
     try:
         return (files(_TEMPLATES_PKG) / f"{name}.md").read_text()
     except FileNotFoundError:
         return None
 
 
+def read_claude_md_primer(shop_type: str) -> str:
+    """Return the canonical CLAUDE.md primer template body for a shop type.
+
+    The body is loaded from package data via importlib.resources; it is
+    never read from a filesystem path under the product working
+    directory. The returned string is the source of truth from which the
+    bootstrap entry point generates a target directory's top-level
+    CLAUDE.md.
+    """
+    if shop_type not in _CANONICAL_ROLE_SETS:
+        raise ValueError(
+            f"unknown shop type {shop_type!r}; accepted values: "
+            f"{', '.join(_SHOP_TYPES)}"
+        )
+    resource = files(_CLAUDE_TEMPLATES_PKG) / f"{shop_type}.md"
+    return resource.read_text()
+
+
+def read_gitignore_template() -> str:
+    """Return the canonical top-level .gitignore template body."""
+    return (files(_TEMPLATES_PKG) / "gitignore.template").read_text()
+
+
+def canonical_role_set(shop_type: str) -> tuple[str, ...]:
+    """Return the canonical role-prompt-template names for a shop type.
+
+    The bootstrap surface treats these names as the canonical role set
+    for the given shop type — both `bootstrap` (initial pour) and
+    `update` (reconciliation) work from this mapping.
+    """
+    if shop_type not in _CANONICAL_ROLE_SETS:
+        raise ValueError(
+            f"unknown shop type {shop_type!r}; accepted values: "
+            f"{', '.join(_SHOP_TYPES)}"
+        )
+    return _CANONICAL_ROLE_SETS[shop_type]
+
+
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
+# `list` / `show` subcommands — pre-existing surface.
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+
 def _cmd_list(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
     for name in _list_template_names():
         print(name)
@@ -60,6 +153,229 @@ def _cmd_show(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
     return 0
 
 
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
+# `bootstrap` subcommand
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+
+def _render_claude_md(shop_type: str, shop_name: str) -> str:
+    """Render the CLAUDE.md primer for a shop type by substituting the
+    shop name. The primer must name the shop's identity (its shop_name
+    and its role set) per scenario a15dac2f87549b8a.
+    """
+    primer = read_claude_md_primer(shop_type)
+    return primer.replace("{{SHOP_NAME}}", shop_name)
+
+
+def _bd_init_in(target: Path) -> int:
+    """Invoke `bd init` as a subprocess in the target directory.
+
+    Per scenario 0c6f1c5d9bc4226e, .beads/ MUST be initialized by a
+    subprocess named "bd" with first argument "init", NOT by importing
+    bd / beads internals into the running shop-templates process and
+    NOT by writing .beads/ files from shop-templates directly. This
+    function is the single place where that subprocess is spawned.
+
+    Per scenario 32d99f6d4a2dad37 (lead-2gr round 2 of lead-k8v's
+    bd-init side-effect closure), `bd init` MUST be invoked with the
+    `--skip-agents` flag. That flag is bd's own opt-out for the
+    AGENTS.md / .claude/settings.json / CLAUDE.md-beads-block append
+    that bd otherwise applies during `bd init`. The shop's canonical
+    agent surface is .claude/agents/*.md alone (poured by bootstrap
+    itself); bd's agent-surface generation is duplicative at best and
+    contradictory at worst, so we suppress it. Verified against bd
+    1.0.3: `BD_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 bd init --skip-agents --quiet` in a
+    fresh probe dir produces .beads/ + .gitignore and no AGENTS.md,
+    no .claude/.
+    """
+    result = subprocess.run(
+        ["bd", "init", "--skip-agents"],
+        cwd=str(target),
+        capture_output=True,
+        text=True,
+    )
+    if result.returncode != 0:
+        print(
+            f"shop-templates bootstrap: `bd init` failed in {target!s} "
+            f"(exit {result.returncode}); stderr:\n{result.stderr}",
+            file=sys.stderr,
+        )
+    return result.returncode
+
+
+def _validate_shop_type(shop_type: str | None, command: str) -> str | None:
+    """Return None if shop_type is valid; else print a usage diagnostic to
+    stderr and return an error sentinel string usable by callers.
+
+    The diagnostic shape is pinned by scenarios d6e935bca62d0039 (missing
+    shop type) and 7941cdc591cd2c3b (invalid shop type): stderr names
+    the offending input AND lists the accepted values "bc" and "lead".
+    """
+    accepted = ", ".join(f'"{s}"' for s in _SHOP_TYPES)
+    if shop_type is None:
+        print(
+            f"shop-templates {command}: missing required argument: shop type. "
+            f"Accepted values: {accepted}.",
+            file=sys.stderr,
+        )
+        return "missing"
+    if shop_type not in _CANONICAL_ROLE_SETS:
+        print(
+            f"shop-templates {command}: invalid shop type {shop_type!r}. "
+            f"Accepted values: {accepted}.",
+            file=sys.stderr,
+        )
+        return "invalid"
+    return None
+
+
+def _cmd_bootstrap(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
+    # Validate shop_type early so we can emit the usage diagnostic shape
+    # the scenarios pin (named offending input + list of accepted values)
+    # BEFORE touching the target directory. Scenarios d6e935bca62d0039
+    # and 7941cdc591cd2c3b both require "writes no scaffold" when the
+    # shop type is bad.
+    err = _validate_shop_type(args.shop_type, "bootstrap")
+    if err is not None:
+        return 2
+
+    target = Path(args.target)
+    shop_type: str = args.shop_type
+    shop_name: str = args.shop_name
+
+    # Pour role-prompt files into .claude/agents/.
+    agents_dir = target / ".claude" / "agents"
+    agents_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
+    for role_name in _CANONICAL_ROLE_SETS[shop_type]:
+        body = _read_template(role_name)
+        if body is None:
+            print(
+                f"shop-templates bootstrap: internal error — canonical "
+                f"role template {role_name!r} not found in package data",
+                file=sys.stderr,
+            )
+            return 1
+        (agents_dir / f"{role_name}.md").write_text(body)
+
+    # Top-level CLAUDE.md — render the primer with the shop's identity.
+    (target / "CLAUDE.md").write_text(_render_claude_md(shop_type, shop_name))
+
+    # Top-level .gitignore — pour the canonical template.
+    (target / ".gitignore").write_text(read_gitignore_template())
+
+    # Initialize .beads/ via a `bd init` subprocess. shop-templates MUST
+    # NOT import bd / beads internals and MUST NOT write to .beads/
+    # directly (scenario 0c6f1c5d9bc4226e).
+    rc = _bd_init_in(target)
+    if rc != 0:
+        return rc
+
+    return 0
+
+
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
+# `update` subcommand
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+
+def _cmd_update(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
+    # Update requires shop_type as an explicit argument so the
+    # reconciliation step (scenario 03b4e3fa31d72031) can compare the
+    # current contents of .claude/agents/ against the current canonical
+    # role set for the right shop type. Inferring the shop type from
+    # .claude/agents/ contents is unsafe: a hand-mutated shop may have
+    # zero files whose names are in either canonical set.
+    err = _validate_shop_type(args.shop_type, "update")
+    if err is not None:
+        return 2
+
+    target = Path(args.target)
+    shop_type: str = args.shop_type
+    agents_dir = target / ".claude" / "agents"
+    agents_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
+
+    canonical = set(_CANONICAL_ROLE_SETS[shop_type])
+
+    # The "naming convention" for bootstrap-managed agent files is:
+    # filename = <role-name>.md where <role-name> is a name in the
+    # canonical role set of SOME shop type (current or historical).
+    # For reconciliation we treat any .md file in .claude/agents/ as a
+    # candidate managed file; if its name is in the current canonical
```

```text
+    # candidate managed file; if its name is in the current canonical
+    # role set for this shop type it stays (and gets re-poured); if
+    # its name is in NEITHER canonical role set we leave it alone
+    # (shop-authored content we don't recognize); if it matches a
+    # canonical name from any role set (including the other shop
+    # type's set, or a name previously in this set but no longer) we
+    # remove it. This satisfies scenario 03b4e3fa31d72031's "remove
+    # managed files whose canonical template no longer exists" while
+    # leaving shop-authored files alone.
+    all_canonical_names = set()
+    for rs in _CANONICAL_ROLE_SETS.values():
+        all_canonical_names.update(rs)
+    # A file is "managed" if its base name (sans .md) is in the
+    # historical set OR is a current canonical name we're about to
+    # pour. For this implementation, treat managed = any .md file
+    # whose name follows the role-prompt-template naming convention,
+    # which we recognize via the heuristic: name contains "-" and is
+    # all lowercase ASCII. That keeps shop-authored files like
+    # "TODO.md" or "NOTES.md" out.
+    def _is_managed_role_name(stem: str) -> bool:
+        # Recognize names of the form "<prefix>-<suffix>" where both
+        # halves are ASCII lowercase letters. This matches every
+        # canonical role-name pattern shipped today (bc-implementer,
+        # bc-reviewer, lead-po, lead-architect) and excludes typical
+        # shop-authored markdown filenames.
+        if "-" not in stem:
+            return False
+        if stem != stem.lower():
+            return False
+        if not all(c.islower() or c == "-" for c in stem):
+            return False
+        return True
+
+    # Step 1: remove managed files whose names are NOT in the current
+    # canonical role set for this shop type.
+    for child in list(agents_dir.iterdir()):
+        if not child.is_file():
+            continue
+        if not child.name.endswith(".md"):
+            continue
+        stem = child.name[:-len(".md")]
+        if stem in canonical:
+            continue
+        if _is_managed_role_name(stem):
+            child.unlink()
+
+    # Step 2: re-pour every canonical role file. If a file is already
+    # byte-equal to the canonical content, leave its on-disk
+    # mtime alone (scenario 264322ae65312bc7).
+    for role_name in canonical:
+        body = _read_template(role_name)
+        if body is None:
+            print(
+                f"shop-templates update: internal error — canonical "
+                f"role template {role_name!r} not found in package data",
+                file=sys.stderr,
+            )
+            return 1
+        target_file = agents_dir / f"{role_name}.md"
+        if target_file.exists():
+            current = target_file.read_text()
+            if current == body:
+                continue
+        target_file.write_text(body)
+
+    # Per scenarios 56a0ac7107ba5c15, ca0f0a249d025267, 3f4d7d2256a97ae7,
+    # and efae77e534588357: update does NOT touch CLAUDE.md, .gitignore,
+    # .beads/, or any path outside .claude/agents/.
+    return 0
+
+

+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
+# Step definitions — inter-shop messaging encapsulation (lead-bwu)
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
+#
+# Scenarios 17-25 of features/inter_shop_messaging_encapsulation.feature
+# pin two invariants across the four canonical templates and the two
+# CLAUDE.md files of the shopsystem product:
+#
+#   1. CLI-only mailbox access: every prescribed inbox/outbox operation
+#      names a `shop-msg` subcommand. No filesystem-direct inspection,
+#      no filename-convention reasoning, no set-difference-on-filenames
+#      characterization of "unprocessed".
+#
+#   2. bd-decoupling: bd participation is the local work-tracker
+#      concern of each shop; no template or CLAUDE.md makes a bd
+#      command a precondition for a shop-msg invocation, and no
+#      template names `--bd-ref` (the flag has been replaced with the
+#      optional, tracker-neutral `--provenance-ref`).
+#
+# Scenarios 21, 22, 25 reach OUTSIDE the BC root to read the lead-shop
+# CLAUDE.md at <product>/CLAUDE.md and the BC's own CLAUDE.md at
+# <product>/repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md. The lead's dispatch
+# (lead-bwu) explicitly names both files as in-scope surfaces — this
+# read is sanctioned by the message.
+
+
+def _product_root() -> Path:
+    """Return the lead-shop (product) root directory.
+
+    The BC root for shopsystem-templates is two levels below the product
+    root: <product>/repos/shopsystem-templates. Resolve by walking up
+    from this file (tests/conftest.py inside the BC) two more levels.
+    """
+    return Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent.parent.parent
+
+
+def _read_file_at_product_path(relative_path: str) -> str:
+    """Read a file path that is given relative to the product root.
+
+    The scenarios spell paths as "CLAUDE.md" (lead shop) and
+    "repos/shopsystem-templates/CLAUDE.md" (BC). Both anchor at the
+    product root.
+    """
+    target = _product_root() / relative_path
+    return target.read_text()
+
+
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
+# Given steps — file-at-path premise (scenarios 21, 22, 25)
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+
+@given(
+    parsers.parse(
+        'the file at "{relative_path}" exists and is the canonical CLAUDE.md '
+        'for the shopsystem-templates BC shop'
+    )
+)
+def given_file_at_path_is_bc_claude_md(relative_path: str, context: dict) -> None:
+    target = _product_root() / relative_path
+    assert target.exists(), (
+        f"premise of Given violated: file {target!s} does not exist"
+    )
+    context["file_path"] = str(target)
+    context["file_relative_path"] = relative_path
+
+
+@given(
+    parsers.parse(
+        'the file at "{relative_path}" exists at the lead shop\'s repository '
+        'root and describes the lead shop\'s router behavior and '
+        'feature-request handling'
+    )
+)
+def given_file_at_path_is_lead_claude_md(relative_path: str, context: dict) -> None:
+    target = _product_root() / relative_path
+    assert target.exists(), (
+        f"premise of Given violated: file {target!s} does not exist"
+    )
+    context["file_path"] = str(target)
+    context["file_relative_path"] = relative_path
+
+
+@given(
+    parsers.parse(
+        'the file at {relative_path} exists and is the canonical CLAUDE.md '
+        'for the named shop'
+    )
+)
+def given_file_at_path_is_named_shop_claude_md(
+    relative_path: str, context: dict
+) -> None:
+    # Scenario Outline (scenario 25) substitutes the path from the

+    relative_path = relative_path.strip()
+    target = _product_root() / relative_path

+        f"premise of Given violated: file {target!s} does not exist"
+    )
+    context["file_path"] = str(target)
+    context["file_relative_path"] = relative_path
+
+
+# -----------------------------------------------------------------------

+
+
+@when("I read that file")
```

```text
2747	        'the target directory additionally contains a non-empty top-level '
2748	        'file at "{path}" authored by the shop'
2749	    )
2750	)
2751	def given_extra_shop_top_level_file(path: str, context: dict) -> None:
2752	    real = _resolve_single_target(context)
2753	    target_file = real / path
2754	    target_file.write_text(f"# shop-authored content at top-level {path}\n")
2755	    extras = context.setdefault("shop_authored_extras", {})
2756	    extras[path] = target_file.read_bytes()
2757	
2758	
2759	@given(
2760	    'I record the byte contents of those four files before the invocation'
2761	)
2762	def given_record_four_files(context: dict) -> None:
2763	    real = _resolve_single_target(context)
2764	    extras = context.get("shop_authored_extras", {})
2765	    snap = {p: (real / p).read_bytes() for p in extras}
2766	    context["pre_invocation_four_files"] = snap
2767	    # Also record all paths pre-invocation so the "no new path other
2768	    # than .claude/agents/" Then step can compare.
2769	    all_paths: set[str] = set()
2770	    for p in real.rglob("*"):
2771	        all_paths.add(str(p.relative_to(real)))
2772	    context["pre_invocation_all_paths"] = all_paths
2773	
2774	
2775	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
2776	# When steps — bootstrap / update invocation
2777	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
2778	
2779	
2780	@when(
2781	    parsers.parse(
2782	        'I invoke the "shop-templates" bootstrap entry point with shop type '
2783	        '"{shop_type}", shop name "{shop_name}", and target directory "{alias}"'
2784	    )
2785	)
2786	def when_invoke_bootstrap(
2787	    shop_type: str,
2788	    shop_name: str,
2789	    alias: str,
2790	    context: dict,
2791	    tmp_path: Path,
2792	) -> None:
2793	    real = _real_target_for_alias(alias, context)
2794	    result = _run_shop_templates_with_bd_shim(
2795	        [
2796	            "bootstrap",
2797	            "--shop-type",
2798	            shop_type,
2799	            "--shop-name",
2800	            shop_name,
2801	            "--target",
2802	            str(real),
2803	        ],
2804	        context,
2805	        tmp_path,
2806	    )
2807	    context["cli_returncode"] = result.returncode
2808	    context["cli_stdout"] = result.stdout
2809	    context["cli_stderr"] = result.stderr
2810	    context["last_invocation_target"] = real
2811	    context["last_invocation_shop_type"] = shop_type
2812	    context["last_invocation_shop_name"] = shop_name
2813	
2814	
2815	@when(
2816	    'I invoke the "shop-templates" bootstrap entry point with a shop name '
2817	    'and a target directory but with no shop type argument'
2818	)
2819	def when_invoke_bootstrap_no_shop_type(
2820	    context: dict, tmp_path: Path
2821	) -> None:
2822	    real = _resolve_single_target(context)
2823	    result = _run_shop_templates_with_bd_shim(
2824	        [
2825	            "bootstrap",
2826	            "--shop-name",
2827	            "example-shop",
2828	            "--target",
2829	            str(real),
2830	        ],
2831	        context,
2832	        tmp_path,
2833	    )
2834	    context["cli_returncode"] = result.returncode
2835	    context["cli_stdout"] = result.stdout
2836	    context["cli_stderr"] = result.stderr
2837	    context["last_invocation_target"] = real
2838	
2839	
2840	@when(
2841	    parsers.parse(
2842	        'I invoke the "shop-templates" update entry point against the '
2843	        'target directory "{alias}"'
2844	    )
2845	)
2846	def when_invoke_update(
2847	    alias: str, context: dict, tmp_path: Path
2848	) -> None:
2849	    real = _real_target_for_alias(alias, context)
2850	    # The shop_type was stashed by the "previously bootstrapped" Given.
2851	    shop_type = context.get("bootstrap_shop_type")
2852	    assert shop_type is not None, (
2853	        "scenario inconsistency: update invoked but no prior bootstrap "
2854	        "captured the shop type"
2855	    )
2856	    result = _run_shop_templates_with_bd_shim(
2857	        [
2858	            "update",
2859	            "--target",
2860	            str(real),
2861	            "--shop-type",
2862	            shop_type,
2863	        ],
2864	        context,
2865	        tmp_path,
2866	    )
2867	    context["cli_returncode"] = result.returncode
2868	    context["cli_stdout"] = result.stdout
2869	    context["cli_stderr"] = result.stderr
2870	    context["last_invocation_target"] = real
2871	
2872	
2873	@when(
2874	    parsers.parse(
2875	        'I ask the "shop-templates" package for the canonical "CLAUDE.md" '
2876	        'primer template for shop type "{shop_type}" through its public '
2877	        'template-access surface'
2878	    )
2879	)
2880	def when_ask_for_claude_primer(shop_type: str, context: dict) -> None:
2881	    from shop_templates.cli import read_claude_md_primer
2882	
2883	    context["claude_primer_shop_type"] = shop_type
2884	    context["claude_primer_body"] = read_claude_md_primer(shop_type)
2885	
2886	
2887	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
2888	# Then steps — bootstrap exit / scaffold presence / non-prompt invariants
2889	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
2890	
2891	
2892	@then(
2893	    'the invocation completes without prompting for any input on stdin'
2894	)
2895	def then_no_stdin_prompt(context: dict) -> None:
2896	    # _run_shop_templates_with_bd_shim runs subprocess.run WITHOUT a
2897	    # stdin=PIPE; if shop-templates tried to read stdin in a terminal-
2898	    # less subprocess context it would EOF immediately rather than
2899	    # hang the test, but we assert it returned with a defined exit
2900	    # code as the empirical demonstration of "did not block".
2901	    rc = context["cli_returncode"]
2902	    assert rc is not None, "invocation did not return an exit code"
2903	
2904	
2905	@then(
2906	    'stderr does not contain any prompt-style text such as "y/n" or '
2907	    '"press enter"'
2908	)
2909	def then_stderr_no_prompt_text(context: dict) -> None:
2910	    stderr = context["cli_stderr"].lower()
2911	    for needle in ("y/n", "press enter", "[y/n]", "(y/n)"):
2912	        assert needle not in stderr, (
2913	            f"unexpected prompt-style text {needle!r} found in stderr:\n"
2914	            f"{context['cli_stderr']}"
2915	        )
2916	
2917	
2918	@then(
2919	    'after the invocation the target directory contains a ".claude/agents/" '
2920	    'directory, a ".beads/" directory, a top-level "CLAUDE.md", and a '
2921	    'top-level ".gitignore"'
2922	)
2923	def then_post_bootstrap_scaffold_present(context: dict) -> None:
2924	    real = context["last_invocation_target"]
2925	    assert (real / ".claude" / "agents").is_dir()
2926	    assert (real / ".beads").is_dir()
2927	    assert (real / "CLAUDE.md").is_file()
2928	    assert (real / ".gitignore").is_file()
2929	
2930	
2931	@then(
2932	    'the target directory still contains no ".claude/agents/" directory '
2933	    'and no ".beads/" directory and no top-level "CLAUDE.md" and no '
2934	    'top-level ".gitignore"'
2935	)
2936	def then_target_still_pristine(context: dict) -> None:
2937	    real = context["last_invocation_target"]
2938	    assert not (real / ".claude" / "agents").exists()
2939	    assert not (real / ".beads").exists()
```

```text
3545	# Step definitions — bootstrap_bd_init_side_effects.feature (lead-2gr)
3546	# =======================================================================
3547	#
3548	# Round 2 of brief 002 §4.4 closure (round 1 was lead-k8v's
3549	# assign_scenarios). The four scenarios pin:
3550	#
3551	#   - 32d99f6d4a2dad37: bootstrap's `bd init` subprocess is invoked
3552	#     with the exact token "--skip-agents".
3553	#   - 7d64bb8ed5a3f656: bootstrap leaves no top-level AGENTS.md and
3554	#     no .claude/settings.json in the target directory.
3555	#   - 5ec07c275350ba81: the canonical CLAUDE.md primer template body
3556	#     contains "bd prime" and at least one bd-subcommand instruction.
3557	#   - 2afb5cba3ea3de25: the bootstrapped target's CLAUDE.md contains
3558	#     the literal substring "bd prime".
3559	#
3560	# Most steps reuse the existing bootstrap/primer infrastructure
3561	# (target alias mapping, bd-shim subprocess logging, primer-access
3562	# `claude_primer_body` context). New steps live below.
3563	
3564	
3565	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
3566	# Given step — AGENTS.md / settings.json absence premise
3567	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
3568	
3569	
3570	@given(
3571	    parsers.parse(
3572	        'an existing git repository at a target directory "{alias}" '
3573	        'containing no top-level "AGENTS.md" and no file at ".claude/settings.json"'
3574	    )
3575	)
3576	def given_existing_git_repo_at_target_no_agents_md_no_settings(
3577	    alias: str, context: dict, tmp_path: Path
3578	) -> None:
3579	    context["bootstrap_workspace"] = tmp_path
3580	    real = _real_target_for_alias(alias, context)
3581	    assert not (real / "AGENTS.md").exists(), (
3582	        f"premise of Given violated: {(real / 'AGENTS.md')!s} unexpectedly exists"
3583	    )
3584	    assert not (real / ".claude" / "settings.json").exists(), (
3585	        f"premise of Given violated: "
3586	        f"{(real / '.claude' / 'settings.json')!s} unexpectedly exists"
3587	    )
3588	
3589	
3590	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
3591	# Then step — bd subprocess argv contains "--skip-agents"
3592	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
3593	
3594	
3595	@then(
3596	    parsers.parse(
3597	        'the argument list passed to that "bd" subprocess contains the '
3598	        'exact token "{token}"'
3599	    )
3600	)
3601	def then_bd_subprocess_argv_contains_token(token: str, context: dict) -> None:
3602	    invocations = _bd_invocations(context)
3603	    assert invocations, (
3604	        "expected at least one `bd` subprocess invocation; bd-shim log is empty"
3605	    )
3606	    # The scenario pairs this Then with the immediately-preceding
3607	    # "subprocess named bd was executed with first argument init" Then,
3608	    # so we check the init invocation specifically. If multiple init
3609	    # invocations are present, all of them must carry the token (the
3610	    # bootstrap CLI spawns exactly one).
3611	    init_invocations = [inv for inv in invocations if inv and inv[0] == "init"]
3612	    assert init_invocations, (
3613	        f"expected a `bd init` invocation; got bd args: "
3614	        f"{[inv[0] if inv else None for inv in invocations]!r}"
3615	    )
3616	    for inv in init_invocations:
3617	        assert token in inv, (
3618	            f"expected token {token!r} in bd init argv {inv!r}; "
3619	            f"not present"
3620	        )
3621	
3622	
3623	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
3624	# Then step — no file at ".claude/settings.json"
3625	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
3626	
3627	
3628	@then(
3629	    parsers.parse(
3630	        'the target directory contains no file at the path "{path}"'
3631	    )
3632	)
3633	def then_target_no_file_at_path(path: str, context: dict) -> None:
3634	    real = context["last_invocation_target"]
3635	    p = real / path
3636	    assert not (p.exists() and p.is_file()), (
3637	        f"target unexpectedly contains file at path {path!r}"
3638	    )
3639	
3640	
3641	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
3642	# Then steps — primer body content (lead-2gr scenario 5ec07c275350ba81)
3643	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
3644	
3645	
3646	@then(
3647	    parsers.parse(
3648	        'the returned body contains the literal substring "{needle}"'
3649	    )
3650	)
3651	def then_primer_body_contains_substring(needle: str, context: dict) -> None:
3652	    body = context["claude_primer_body"]
3653	    assert needle in body, (
3654	        f"primer body does not contain literal substring {needle!r}"
3655	    )
3656	
3657	
3658	@then(
3659	    'the returned body contains at least one instruction directing the '
3660	    'reader to run a "bd" subcommand as part of the shop\'s working '
3661	    'discipline'
3662	)
3663	def then_primer_body_directs_bd_subcommand(context: dict) -> None:
3664	    # The scenario requires *at least one instruction directing the
3665	    # reader to run a "bd" subcommand*. We interpret that as: the body
3666	    # mentions a bd subcommand invocation in an instructional context —
3667	    # operationally, the literal substring "bd " (bd followed by a
3668	    # space, i.e. a subcommand follows) appears, AND it appears in a
3669	    # context other than the bare phrase "bd prime" alone (so the
3670	    # body really does direct the reader to a bd subcommand command
3671	    # surface, not just name-drop "bd prime" once).
3672	    body = context["claude_primer_body"]
3673	    # Find every occurrence of "bd <word>" in the body and collect the
3674	    # set of subcommand tokens that follow "bd ".
3675	    import re
3676	    matches = re.findall(r"\bbd ([a-z][a-z\-]*)\b", body)
3677	    assert matches, (
3678	        f"primer body does not contain any 'bd <subcommand>' instruction; "
3679	        f"body:\n{body!r}"
3680	    )
3681	    # At least one *non-empty* subcommand must be present. (re's
3682	    # character class above already requires at least one lowercase
3683	    # letter, so the set is non-empty if matches is non-empty.)
3684	    subcommands = set(matches)
3685	    assert subcommands, (
3686	        "primer body mentions 'bd' but no subcommand follows it"
3687	    )
3688
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>

```text
1	# {{SHOP_NAME}} — BC shop instructions
2	
3	This repository is the **{{SHOP_NAME}}** Bounded Context shop. As an agent
4	operating in this repo, you are operating inside a **BC shop** that uses
5	the inbox/outbox message protocol from §4 of the shop-system spec.
6	
7	## Who you are — router for bc-implementer and bc-reviewer subagents
8	
9	By default you are the **router** for this BC shop. The two role-discipline
10	positions — **Implementer** and **Reviewer** per the shop-system spec §4 /
11	§4.4 — are dispatched as subagents. Your job is to classify each request
12	and delegate; do not enact the roles yourself.
13	
14	The canonical role set for this shop type is:
15	
16	- **bc-implementer** — reads inbox messages, applies the sufficiency check
17	  matching the message type, and either emits `clarify` via `shop-msg
18	  respond clarify` or does the work (feature file under `features/`, step
19	  defs in `tests/conftest.py`, implementation under `src/`, BDD passing).
20	
21	- **bc-reviewer** — dispatched AFTER the implementer's turn on an
22	  `assign_scenarios` (or scenario-carrying `request_bugfix`) message has
23	  finished and the BC is in its post-work state with no outbox file yet.
24	  The reviewer is the sole role authorized to emit `work_done` for
25	  scenario-based work.
26	
27	Subagent definitions live at `.claude/agents/bc-implementer.md` and
28	`.claude/agents/bc-reviewer.md`. These are inline copies of the canonical
29	templates shipped by the `shopsystem-templates` BC; do not edit them
30	independently of the canonical source.
31	
32	## BC inbox / outbox protocol
33	
34	- **Inbox** (`inbox/`) holds messages from the lead shop. Filename
35	  convention: `<work_id>.yaml`. One file per dispatch.
36	- **Outbox** (`outbox/`) holds this BC's responses. Filename convention:
37	  `<work_id>-<response_type>.yaml`. The `shop-msg respond` CLI builds
38	  and validates these — never write outbox YAML by hand.
39	- A message is considered **unprocessed** when there is no outbox file
40	  for its `work_id`. Use `shop-msg pending inbox` to identify
41	  unprocessed work, and `shop-msg read inbox` to read a specific
42	  inbox message.
43	
44	## Do not
45	
46	- **No editing the BC's inbox/outbox by hand.** `shop-msg send` writes
47	  inboxes (lead shop's job); `shop-msg respond` writes outboxes
48	  (BC's job). Both validate against the schema.
49	- **No skipping the sufficiency check.** Each BC role template carries
50	  a sufficiency check matching the inbound message type; honor it.
51	
52	## Beads (bd) discipline
53	
54	This shop uses **bd (beads)** for its work-tracking registry. The
55	inbound `work_id` on each inbox message is the lead shop's bead ID;
56	this BC's own follow-up findings (mechanism observations, escaped
57	risks, deferred work) are filed as beads in this repo.
58	
59	- Run `bd prime` at the start of a working session to load the full
60	  workflow context and command reference for this repo.
61	- Use `bd ready` to find available work, `bd show <id>` to inspect
62	  an issue, and `bd close <id>` to mark work complete. Do NOT track
63	  work in markdown TODO lists or alternative trackers.
64	- Use `bd remember` for persistent knowledge that should outlive the
65	  session.
66
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-13 15:16:30Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>

 `./src/shop_templates/templates/claude/lead.md`

```text
1	# {{SHOP_NAME}} — lead shop instructions
2	
3	This repository is the **{{SHOP_NAME}}** lead shop. As an agent operating
4	in this repo, you are operating inside a **lead shop** under the shop-system
5	spec.
6	
7	## Who you are — router for lead-po and lead-architect subagents
8	
9	By default you are the **router** for this lead shop. The two judgment
10	roles — **PO** and **Architect** per §3 of the shop-system spec — are
11	dispatched as subagents. Your job is to classify each request and
12	delegate; do not enact the roles yourself.
13	
14	The canonical role set for this shop type is:
15	
16	- **lead-po** — authors or sharpens Gherkin scenarios; drafts briefs or
17	  PDRs; responds to BC `clarify` on scope or vocabulary.
18	
19	- **lead-architect** — selects a message-type vehicle; composes
20	  `shop-msg send`; verifies BC pre-state empirically; responds to BC
21	  `clarify` on architecture; reconciles scenario registers; drafts ADRs;
22	  makes BC decomposition decisions.
23	
24	Subagent definitions live at `.claude/agents/lead-po.md` and
25	`.claude/agents/lead-architect.md`. These are inline copies of the
26	canonical templates shipped by the `shopsystem-templates` BC; do not
27	edit them independently of the canonical source.
28	
29	## How feature requests get handled
30	
31	When a user request implies a new BC capability, a tightening, or a flat
32	change to an existing BC:
33	
34	1. **PO authors intent first.** Brief → PDR → Gherkin scenarios.
35	   Implementation discussion before authored scenarios is the failure
36	   mode §3 exists to prevent.
37	2. **Architect verifies the relevant BC's pre-state empirically.**
38	   Reading the BC's code is hypothesis; running it is fact. Construct a
39	   concrete input that exhibits (or fails to exhibit) the behavior;
40	   observe; cite the demonstration in the dispatch description.
41	3. **Architect applies the message-type discriminator:**
42	   - No capability → `assign_scenarios`.
43	   - Capability exists but unpinned → `request_bugfix`.
44	   - Flat (refactor / doc / value-only) → `request_maintenance`.
45	4. **Architect dispatches via `shop-msg send`.** Never write inbox/outbox
46	   YAML by hand.
47	
48	## Do not
49	
50	- **No implementation code in this repo.** This is the lead shop; code
51	  lives in BCs.
52	- **No skipping the discriminator.** If a request smells like open-ended
53	  "what should we build?" — route it back through the process. First
54	  question: *what scenarios pin this?*
55	
56	## Beads (bd) discipline
57	
58	This shop uses **bd (beads)** for its work-tracking registry. Lead-shop
59	bead IDs are the canonical `work_id` values that flow outward into
60	`shop-msg send` when dispatching to a BC.
61	
62	- Run `bd prime` at the start of a working session to load the full
63	  workflow context and command reference for this repo.
64	- Use `bd ready` to find available work, `bd show <id>` to inspect
65	  an issue, `bd update <id> --claim` to claim work, and `bd close <id>`
66	  to mark work complete. Do NOT track work in markdown TODO lists or
67	  alternative trackers.
68	- Use `bd remember` for persistent knowledge that should outlive the
69	  session.
70
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
2150	    return tmp_path
2151	
2152	
2153	def _make_bd_shim_dir(tmp_path: Path, context: dict) -> Path:
2154	    """Create a directory containing a fake `bd` executable that logs
2155	    invocations to a file and minimally simulates `bd init` (mkdir
2156	    .beads/ in cwd with a marker file inside). Return the directory
2157	    path so the caller can prepend it to PATH.
2158	
2159	    Per scenarios 0c6f1c5d9bc4226e and 3f4d7d2256a97ae7, tests need to
2160	    answer "did a `bd` subprocess run, and with what first argument".
2161	    The log file path is stashed on context as "bd_shim_log" and is
2162	    TRUNCATED on each call — so only the most-recent invocation's bd
2163	    calls are observable. (Tests that need to chain a Given-side
2164	    bootstrap into a When-side update don't want the Given-side `bd
2165	    init` call to pollute the When-side observation window.)
2166	    """
2167	    shim_dir = tmp_path / "bd-shim"
2168	    shim_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
2169	    log = tmp_path / "bd-shim.log"
2170	    # Truncate so each invocation observes only its own subprocess calls.
2171	    log.write_text("")
2172	    context["bd_shim_log"] = log
2173	    bd_path = shim_dir / "bd"
2174	    # The shim is intentionally tiny — it logs argv (one line per call:
2175	    # "<arg1> <arg2> ..." plus its cwd) and, if the first arg is "init",
2176	    # creates ".beads/__shim_init__" inside cwd as the visible
2177	    # side-effect bootstrap callers depend on.
2178	    bd_path.write_text(
2179	        "#!/usr/bin/env bash\n"
2180	        f"log={log}\n"
2181	        'echo "$@|cwd=$PWD" >> "$log"\n'
2182	        'if [[ "$1" == "init" ]]; then\n'
2183	        '  mkdir -p .beads\n'
2184	        '  : > .beads/__shim_init__\n'
2185	        'fi\n'
2186	        "exit 0\n"
2187	    )
2188	    bd_path.chmod(0o755)
2189	    return shim_dir
2190	
2191	
2192	def _run_shop_templates_with_bd_shim(
2193	    args: list[str], context: dict, tmp_path: Path
2194	) -> subprocess.CompletedProcess:
2195	    """Invoke `shop-templates` with a fake `bd` on PATH, capturing
2196	    whether/how `bd` was called and whether `bd`/`beads` Python modules
2197	    got imported into the shop-templates process.
2198	
2199	    The "no bd/beads imports" check (scenario 0c6f1c5d9bc4226e) is
2200	    implemented by invoking shop-templates indirectly via a small
2201	    Python wrapper that (1) runs `shop_templates.cli.main(args)` and
2202	    (2) after main returns, writes `bd_in_modules=True/False` and
2203	    `beads_in_modules=True/False` to an import-trace log. The test
2204	    asserts both are False.
2205	    """
2206	    import os
2207	    import shutil
2208	
2209	    shim_dir = _make_bd_shim_dir(tmp_path, context)
2210	    import_log = tmp_path / "import-trace.log"
2211	    context["import_trace_log"] = import_log
2212	
2213	    # Build a wrapper script that runs shop_templates.cli.main(argv)
2214	    # and then writes the import-trace flags. We must NOT import bd
2215	    # or beads ourselves anywhere in this wrapper (the assertion would
2216	    # then trivially fail).
2217	    wrapper = tmp_path / "_run_shop_templates_wrapper.py"
2218	    args_repr = repr(list(args))
2219	    wrapper.write_text(
2220	        "import sys\n"
2221	        "from shop_templates.cli import main\n"
2222	        f"argv = {args_repr}\n"
2223	        "rc = main(argv)\n"
2224	        "import_log = open(r%r, 'w')\n" % str(import_log)
2225	        + "bd_in = any(\n"
2226	        "    m == 'bd' or m.startswith('bd.')\n"
2227	        "    for m in sys.modules\n"
2228	        ")\n"
2229	        "beads_in = any(\n"
2230	        "    m == 'beads' or m.startswith('beads.')\n"
2231	        "    for m in sys.modules\n"
2232	        ")\n"
2233	        "import_log.write(f'bd_in_modules={bd_in}\\n')\n"
2234	        "import_log.write(f'beads_in_modules={beads_in}\\n')\n"
2235	        "import_log.close()\n"
2236	        "sys.exit(rc)\n"
2237	    )
2238	
2239	    env = os.environ.copy()
2240	    env["PATH"] = f"{shim_dir}{os.pathsep}{env.get('PATH', '')}"
2241	
2242	    # Use the same python interpreter that's running the test, so
2243	    # `shop_templates` is importable from the installed package.
2244	    py = sys.executable
2245	    return subprocess.run(
2246	        [py, str(wrapper)],
2247	        capture_output=True,
2248	        text=True,
2249	        env=env,
2250	    )
2251	
2252

2254	# Given steps — target directory premise
2255	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------

2261	    )

2265	) -> None:
2266	    context["bootstrap_workspace"] = tmp_path
2267	    _real_target_for_alias(alias, context)
```

```text
3119	def _bd_invocations(context: dict) -> list[list[str]]:
3120	    """Return a list of bd-shim invocation argvs from the per-test log,
3121	    or [] if the log doesn't exist (the shim was never invoked)."""
3122	    log = context.get("bd_shim_log")
3123	    if log is None or not log.exists():
3124	        return []
3125	    invocations: list[list[str]] = []
3126	    for line in log.read_text().splitlines():
3127	        if not line.strip():
3128	            continue
3129	        # Format: "<arg1> <arg2> ...|cwd=<cwd>"
3130	        argline = line.split("|cwd=", 1)[0]
3131	        invocations.append(argline.split())
3132	    return invocations
3133

3147	    )

3152	    'package name is "bd" or "beads" was imported into the running '
3153	    '"shop-templates" process'
3154	)

3174	def then_beads_written_by_bd_subprocess(context: dict) -> None:
```

### Probe 2: `bd init --skip-agents` works against real bd 1.0.3 — verified empirically that `--skip-agents` exists. Done.

### Probe 3: Does the bd-subcommand test (`then_primer_body_directs_bd_subcommand`) have teeth, or could it pass with just literal `bd prime`?

Looking at the regex: `r"\bbd ([a-z][a-z\-]*)\b"`. With only "bd prime" in body, it matches `["prime"]`, asserts `matches` non-empty (passes), and asserts subcommands non-empty (passes). So **this Then alone would pass with just `bd prime` appearing once** — the "directs at least one bd subcommand" assertion is degenerate.

But wait — the **scenario** has BOTH "contains literal substring bd prime" AND "contains at least one bd subcommand instruction". If only `bd prime` is present, the bd-subcommand check passes (it parses out "prime"). Is "prime" a valid subcommand? Yes (`bd prime` is one of bd's commands). So actually this is fine *structurally* — the assertion does match exactly what `bd prime` looks like.

But the issue is the **redundancy** — the second Then doesn't add teeth beyond the first. The implementer comment even acknowledges this concern ("not just name-drop 'bd prime' once"), but then the implementation actually *does* permit `bd prime` to satisfy both. Let me probe this directly: remove every other bd-subcommand reference but keep `bd prime`.

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-13 15:17:39Z)**_

Confirmed: with **only `bd prime`** in bc.md and no other bd-subcommand instructions, scenario `5ec07c275350ba81[bc]` **still passes**. The bd-subcommand "at least one instruction" check is degenerate — it's satisfied by the same `bd prime` token that satisfies the `bd prime` substring check.

Is this a scenario gap or an implementation gap? Re-reading scenario `5ec07c275350ba81`:

> Then a non-empty template body is returned
> And the returned body contains the literal substring "bd prime"
> And the returned body contains at least one instruction directing the reader to run a "bd" subcommand as part of the shop's working discipline

These are TWO separate Thens. The reasonable reading is "bd prime" is one substring check AND there is at least one *additional* bd-subcommand instruction. But the scenario doesn't say "in addition to bd prime" — and `bd prime` IS literally an instruction directing the reader to run a bd subcommand (`prime`).

Looking at the wire: the scenario "names 'bd prime' as the entry point" and *additionally* says "at least one instruction directing the reader to run a bd subcommand." The PO clearly meant `bd prime` is the entry point AND there is at least one further instruction. The implementer interpretation that `bd prime` itself satisfies the "at least one bd subcommand instruction" is technically allowable on the literal text but probably misses intent.

However: **is this a behaviorally important gap?** The current bc.md and lead.md both contain *multiple* bd-subcommand instructions (`bd ready`, `bd show`, `bd close`, `bd remember`, `bd update --claim`). Removing them would be regressive — and *not pinned*. A future change that strips them would silently pass. This is a real scenario gap.

Restore.

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-13 15:17:52Z)**_

But wait — let me re-read the PO's commitment in the dispatch description: *"(b) the canonical CLAUDE.md primer templates that bootstrap consumes carry bd-discipline references (bd prime substring + at least one bd-subcommand instruction) directly"* — singular "at least one bd-subcommand instruction." The PO explicitly chose "at least one." The scenario faithfully encodes that. So the implementer's reading IS what the scenario actually pins. The scenario gap question is: did PO intend "at least one bd-subcommand OTHER than `prime`" or was `prime` itself good enough?

Given the PO wrote the brief as "bd prime substring + at least one bd-subcommand instruction" — it does read as two separable commitments. **But the scenario itself does not exclude `bd prime` from satisfying the second commitment.** Strictly, this is a scenario gap. The implementer realized the spec as-written, faithfully — they didn't shortcut. The gap is on the scenario.

This is borderline. Let me weigh: does it pass a reasonable implementation? Yes — a reasonable implementation (any future canonical primer with at least one named bd-subcommand instruction other than `bd prime`) passes the scenario. Does it leave a regression unguarded? Yes — a future tightening primer that stripped all bd-discipline instructions except `bd prime` would also pass.

This **could** be a §4.4 clarify candidate. But I should also weigh: the PO's resolution in the dispatch description is *committed* — they explicitly said "bd prime substring + at least one bd-subcommand instruction" as a deliberate two-piece commitment. The "+at least one" was the deliberate scope, NOT "at least one *other than* prime". The PO may have decided this is sufficient for the round 2 closure. Asking for tightening would be re-opening a deliberately committed scope.

I'll surface this in my summary as a probe I considered. The implementer's realization is faithful to the literal scenario AND to the PO's stated commitment in the dispatch. The "what if future primer strips all bd-subcommands except prime" risk is real but unpinned, and unpinning that further has diminishing returns: the primer also explicitly has scenario `2afb5cba3ea3de25` for the target-dir CLAUDE.md content. I'll dismiss the probe as out-of-scope for this round — not raise a clarify.

## Reviewer report — lead-2gr

**Outcome:** sign-off (`work_done` status=complete) via `shop-msg respond work_done`. Outbox: `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/repos/shopsystem-templates/outbox/lead-2gr-work_done.yaml`.

**BDD re-run:** 105 passed, 0 failed (97 baseline + 8 new outline rows, matching the implementer's claim). Note: initial `pytest` invocation under bare bash showed 33 fails because `shop-templates` was not on the shell's `PATH`; rerun with the product venv activated produced the clean 105/105.

**Scenario hashes echoed back:** `32d99f6d4a2dad37`, `7d64bb8ed5a3f656`, `5ec07c275350ba81`, `2afb5cba3ea3de25` (new), plus `0c6f1c5d9bc4226e` (pre-existing pin the new work is additive to — still passes, confirming `--skip-agents` did not regress the "subprocess named bd, first arg init" check).

**Adversarial probes:**

1. **Mutation: drop `--skip-agents` from `_bd_init_in`.** Both `32d99f6d4a2dad37` rows fail. Pin has teeth.
2. **Mutation: bootstrap writes a bogus `AGENTS.md` and `.claude/settings.json`.** Both `7d64bb8ed5a3f656` rows fail. Pin has teeth.
3. **Mutation: strip `bd prime` from `templates/claude/bc.md`.** `5ec07c275350ba81[bc]` and `2afb5cba3ea3de25[bc]` fail. Both substring pins have teeth.
4. **Real-bd-1.0.3 check.** `bd init --help` lists `--skip-agents`; empirical run in `/tmp/bd-probe` with `BD_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 bd init --skip-agents --quiet` produces `.beads/` + `.gitignore` and no `AGENTS.md`, no `.claude/`. PO's commitment is sound.
5. **bd-subcommand "at least one instruction" check degeneracy.** The regex `\bbd ([a-z][a-z-]*)\b` parses `prime` from `bd prime` as a subcommand — so the second Then on `5ec07c27` is satisfied by the *same* `bd prime` token that satisfies the first Then. Confirmed by mutation (bc.md with only `bd prime`, no other bd-subcommand instructions, still passes). **Dismissed** as faithful to PO's committed scope: the dispatch description explicitly frames the commitment as "bd prime substring + at least one bd-subcommand instruction" and `prime` IS a bd subcommand. Raising clarify would re-open a deliberately committed resolution.
6. **Hash discipline.** All four dispatched wire-form gherkins canonicalize via `scenarios hash` to their claimed hashes; all four on-disk scenarios in `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/repos/shopsystem-templates/features/bootstrap_bd_init_side_effects.feature` canonicalize identically. `@scenario_hash:` and `@bc:shopsystem-templates` tags preserved exactly per probe-4.
7. **Inline subagent staleness.** Not relevant — lead-2gr touched only `src/shop_templates/cli.py`, `src/shop_templates/templates/claude/{bc,lead}.md`, `tests/conftest.py`, and `features/bootstrap_bd_init_side_effects.feature`. Pre-existing `.claude/agents/bc-{implementer,reviewer}.md` modifications in `git status` are from a different in-flight session (lead-bwu/lead-e4g still have open clarify outboxes).

**No `mechanism_observation` emitted.** Probe 5 surfaced a degeneracy that I dismissed as in-scope-for-PO; it is not a mechanism-level pattern about the reviewer-template or implementer-template anti-rationalization language.

_**User (2026-05-13 15:51:00Z)**_

# /loop — schedule a recurring or self-paced prompt

Parse the input below into `[interval] <prompt…>` and schedule it.

## Parsing (in priority order)

1. **Leading token**: if the first whitespace-delimited token matches `^\d+[smhd]$` (e.g. `5m`, `2h`), that's the interval; the rest is the prompt.
2. **Trailing "every" clause**: otherwise, if the input ends with `every <N><unit>` or `every <N> <unit-word>` (e.g. `every 20m`, `every 5 minutes`, `every 2 hours`), extract that as the interval and strip it from the prompt. Only match when what follows "every" is a time expression — `check every PR` has no interval.
3. **No interval**: otherwise, the entire input is the prompt and you'll self-pace dynamically (see "Dynamic mode" below).

If the resulting prompt is empty, show usage `/loop [interval] <prompt>` and stop.

Examples:
- `5m /babysit-prs` → interval `5m`, prompt `/babysit-prs` (rule 1)
- `check the deploy every 20m` → interval `20m`, prompt `check the deploy` (rule 2)
- `run tests every 5 minutes` → interval `5m`, prompt `run tests` (rule 2)
- `check the deploy` → no interval → dynamic mode, prompt `check the deploy` (rule 3)
- `check every PR` → no interval → dynamic mode, prompt `check every PR` (rule 3 — "every" not followed by time)
- `5m` → empty prompt → show usage

## Offer cloud first

Before any scheduling step, check whether EITHER is true:
- the parsed interval (rule 1 or 2) is **≥60 minutes**, or
- regardless of which rule matched, the original input uses daily phrasing ("every morning", "daily", "every day", "each night", "every weekday")

If either is true, call AskUserQuestion first:
- `question`: "This loop stops when you close this session. Set it up as a cloud schedule instead so it keeps running?"
- `header`: "Schedule"
- `options`: `[{label: "Cloud schedule (recommended)", description: "Runs in Anthropic's cloud even after you close this session"}, {label: "This session only", description: "Runs in this terminal until you exit"}]`

If they pick **Cloud schedule**: do NOT call CronCreate. Invoke the `schedule` skill directly via the Skill tool with `args` set to their original input verbatim (e.g. `Skill({skill: "schedule", args: "every morning tell me a joke"})`), then follow that skill's instructions to completion. Do NOT tell the user to run /schedule themselves. **Then stop — do not continue to any section below** (no CronCreate, no ScheduleWakeup, no "execute the prompt now").
If they pick **This session only**:
- If the trigger was a parsed ≥60-minute interval (rule 1 or 2): continue below with that interval.
- If the trigger was daily phrasing only (rule 3, no parsed interval): do NOT call CronCreate. Explain that a daily-cadence loop won't fire before this session closes, so there's nothing useful to schedule locally — suggest they either pick Cloud schedule, or re-run `/loop` with an explicit shorter interval (e.g. `/loop 1h <prompt>`) if they want a session loop. Then stop.
If neither trigger condition was met: continue below.

## Fixed-interval mode (rules 1 and 2)

Convert the interval to a cron expression:

| Interval pattern      | Cron expression     | Notes                                    |
|-----------------------|---------------------|------------------------------------------|
| `Nm` where N ≤ 59   | `*/N * * * *`     | every N minutes                          |
| `Nm` where N ≥ 60   | `0 */H * * *`     | round to hours (H = N/60, must divide 24)|
| `Nh` where N ≤ 23   | `0 */N * * *`     | every N hours                            |
| `Nd`                | `0 0 */N * *`     | every N days at midnight local           |
| `Ns`                | treat as `ceil(N/60)m` | cron minimum granularity is 1 minute  |

**If the interval doesn't cleanly divide its unit** (e.g. `7m` → `*/7 * * * *` gives uneven gaps at :56→:00; `90m` → 1.5h which cron can't express), pick the nearest clean interval and tell the user what you rounded to before scheduling.

Then:
1. Call CronCreate with: `cron` (the expression above), `prompt` (the parsed prompt verbatim), `recurring: true`.
2. Briefly confirm: what's scheduled, the cron expression, the human-readable cadence, that recurring tasks auto-expire after 7 days, and that the user can cancel sooner with CronDelete (include the job ID). Only if you did NOT show the cloud-offer AskUserQuestion above (i.e., neither trigger condition applied), end the confirmation with this exact line on its own, italicized: `_Runs until you close this session · For durable cloud-based loops, use /schedule_`. If the user already answered that question, omit this line.
3. **Then immediately execute the parsed prompt now** — don't wait for the first cron fire. If it's a slash command, invoke it via the Skill tool; otherwise act on it directly.

## Dynamic mode (rule 3 — no interval)

The user wants you to self-pace. Decide what makes the next iteration worth running — a passage of time, or an observable event.

1. **Run the parsed prompt now.** If it's a slash command, invoke it via the Skill tool; otherwise act on it directly.
2. **If the next run is gated on an event** (CI finishing, a log line matching, a file changing, a PR comment) and no Monitor is already running for it: arm one now with `persistent: true`. Its events arrive as `<task-notification>` messages and wake this loop immediately — you do not wait for the ScheduleWakeup deadline. Arm once; on later iterations call TaskList first and skip this step if a monitor is already running.
3. **Briefly confirm**: that you're self-pacing, whether a Monitor is the primary wake signal, that you ran the task now, and what fallback delay you're about to pick. Write this as text *before* calling ScheduleWakeup — the turn ends as soon as that tool returns.
4. **Then, as the last action of this turn, call ScheduleWakeup** with:
   - `delaySeconds`: with a Monitor armed this is the **fallback heartbeat** — how long to wait if no event fires (lean 1200–1800s; idle ticks past the 5-minute cache window are pure overhead). Without a Monitor this is the cadence — pick based on what you observed. Read the tool's own description for cache-aware delay guidance.
   - `reason`: one short sentence on why you picked that delay.
   - `prompt`: the full original /loop input verbatim, prefixed with `/loop ` so the next firing re-enters this skill and continues the loop. For example, if the user typed `/loop check the deploy`, pass `/loop check the deploy` as the prompt.
5. **If you were woken by a `<task-notification>`** rather than this prompt: handle the event in the context of the loop task, then call ScheduleWakeup again with the same `prompt` and the same 1200–1800s `delaySeconds` from step 4 — the Monitor remains the wake signal; this only resets the safety net.
6. **To stop the loop**, omit the ScheduleWakeup call and TaskStop any Monitor you armed (use TaskList to find the task ID if it is no longer in context).

Its events arrive as `<task-notification>` messages and wake this loop immediately — you do not wait for the ScheduleWakeup deadline. Arm once; on later iterations call TaskList first and skip this step if a monitor is already running.
3. **Briefly confirm**: that you're self-pacing, whether a Monitor is the primary wake signal, that you ran the task now, and what fallback delay you're about to pick. Write this as text *before* calling ScheduleWakeup — the turn ends as soon as that tool returns.
4. **Then, as the last action of this turn, call ScheduleWakeup** with:
   - `delaySeconds`: with a Monitor armed this is the **fallback heartbeat** — how long to wait if no event fires (lean 1200–1800s; idle ticks past the 5-minute cache window are pure overhead). Without a Monitor this is the cadence — pick based on what you observed. Read the tool's own description for cache-aware delay guidance.
   - `reason`: one short sentence on why you picked that delay.
   - `prompt`: the full original /loop input verbatim, prefixed with `/loop ` so the next firing re-enters this skill and continues the loop. For example, if the user typed `/loop check the deploy`, pass `/loop check the deploy` as the prompt.
5. **If you were woken by a `<task-notification>`** rather than this prompt: handle the event in the context of the loop task, then call ScheduleWakeup again with the same `prompt` and the same 1200–1800s `delaySeconds` from step 4 — the Monitor remains the wake signal; this only resets the safety net.
6. **To stop the loop**, omit the ScheduleWakeup call and TaskStop any Monitor you armed (use TaskList to find the task ID if it is no longer in context).

# /loop — schedule a recurring or self-paced prompt

Parse the input below into `[interval] <prompt…>` and schedule it.

## Parsing (in priority order)

1. **Leading token**: if the first whitespace-delimited token matches `^\d+[smhd]$` (e.g. `5m`, `2h`), that's the interval; the rest is the prompt.
2. **Trailing "every" clause**: otherwise, if the input ends with `every <N><unit>` or `every <N> <unit-word>` (e.g. `every 20m`, `every 5 minutes`, `every 2 hours`), extract that as the interval and strip it from the prompt. Only match when what follows "every" is a time expression — `check every PR` has no interval.
3. **No interval**: otherwise, the entire input is the prompt and you'll self-pace dynamically (see "Dynamic mode" below).

If the resulting prompt is empty, show usage `/loop [interval] <prompt>` and stop.

Examples:
- `5m /babysit-prs` → interval `5m`, prompt `/babysit-prs` (rule 1)
- `check the deploy every 20m` → interval `20m`, prompt `check the deploy` (rule 2)
- `run tests every 5 minutes` → interval `5m`, prompt `run tests` (rule 2)
- `check the deploy` → no interval → dynamic mode, prompt `check the deploy` (rule 3)
- `check every PR` → no interval → dynamic mode, prompt `check every PR` (rule 3 — "every" not followed by time)
- `5m` → empty prompt → show usage

## Offer cloud first

Before any scheduling step, check whether EITHER is true:
- the parsed interval (rule 1 or 2) is **≥60 minutes**, or
- regardless of which rule matched, the original input uses daily phrasing ("every morning", "daily", "every day", "each night", "every weekday")

If either is true, call AskUserQuestion first:
- `question`: "This loop stops when you close this session. Set it up as a cloud schedule instead so it keeps running?"
- `header`: "Schedule"
- `options`: `[{label: "Cloud schedule (recommended)", description: "Runs in Anthropic's cloud even after you close this session"}, {label: "This session only", description: "Runs in this terminal until you exit"}]`

If they pick **Cloud schedule**: do NOT call CronCreate. Invoke the `schedule` skill directly via the Skill tool with `args` set to their original input verbatim (e.g. `Skill({skill: "schedule", args: "every morning tell me a joke"})`), then follow that skill's instructions to completion. Do NOT tell the user to run /schedule themselves. **Then stop — do not continue to any section below** (no CronCreate, no ScheduleWakeup, no "execute the prompt now").
If they pick **This session only**:
- If the trigger was a parsed ≥60-minute interval (rule 1 or 2): continue below with that interval.
- If the trigger was daily phrasing only (rule 3, no parsed interval): do NOT call CronCreate. Explain that a daily-cadence loop won't fire before this session closes, so there's nothing useful to schedule locally — suggest they either pick Cloud schedule, or re-run `/loop` with an explicit shorter interval (e.g. `/loop 1h <prompt>`) if they want a session loop. Then stop.
If neither trigger condition was met: continue below.

## Fixed-interval mode (rules 1 and 2)

Convert the interval to a cron expression:

| Interval pattern      | Cron expression     | Notes                                    |
|-----------------------|---------------------|------------------------------------------|
| `Nm` where N ≤ 59   | `*/N * * * *`     | every N minutes                          |
| `Nm` where N ≥ 60   | `0 */H * * *`     | round to hours (H = N/60, must divide 24)|
| `Nh` where N ≤ 23   | `0 */N * * *`     | every N hours                            |
| `Nd`                | `0 0 */N * *`     | every N days at midnight local           |
| `Ns`                | treat as `ceil(N/60)m` | cron minimum granularity is 1 minute  |

**If the interval doesn't cleanly divide its unit** (e.g. `7m` → `*/7 * * * *` gives uneven gaps at :56→:00; `90m` → 1.5h which cron can't express), pick the nearest clean interval and tell the user what you rounded to before scheduling.

Then:
1. Call CronCreate with: `cron` (the expression above), `prompt` (the parsed prompt verbatim), `recurring: true`.
2. Briefly confirm: what's scheduled, the cron expression, the human-readable cadence, that recurring tasks auto-expire after 7 days, and that the user can cancel sooner with CronDelete (include the job ID). Only if you did NOT show the cloud-offer AskUserQuestion above (i.e., neither trigger condition applied), end the confirmation with this exact line on its own, italicized: `_Runs until you close this session · For durable cloud-based loops, use /schedule_`. If the user already answered that question, omit this line.
3. **Then immediately execute the parsed prompt now** — don't wait for the first cron fire. If it's a slash command, invoke it via the Skill tool; otherwise act on it directly.

## Dynamic mode (rule 3 — no interval)

The user wants you to self-pace. Decide what makes the next iteration worth running — a passage of time, or an observable event.

1. **Run the parsed prompt now.** If it's a slash command, invoke it via the Skill tool; otherwise act on it directly.
2. **If the next run is gated on an event** (CI finishing, a log line matching, a file changing, a PR comment) and no Monitor is already running for it: arm one now with `persistent: true`. Its events arrive as `<task-notification>` messages and wake this loop immediately — you do not wait for the ScheduleWakeup deadline. Arm once; on later iterations call TaskList first and skip this step if a monitor is already running.
3. **Briefly confirm**: that you're self-pacing, whether a Monitor is the primary wake signal, that you ran the task now, and what fallback delay you're about to pick. Write this as text *before* calling ScheduleWakeup — the turn ends as soon as that tool returns.
4. **Then, as the last action of this turn, call ScheduleWakeup** with:
   - `delaySeconds`: with a Monitor armed this is the **fallback heartbeat** — how long to wait if no event fires (lean 1200–1800s; idle ticks past the 5-minute cache window are pure overhead). Without a Monitor this is the cadence — pick based on what you observed. Read the tool's own description for cache-aware delay guidance.
   - `reason`: one short sentence on why you picked that delay.
   - `prompt`: the full original /loop input verbatim, prefixed with `/loop ` so the next firing re-enters this skill and continues the loop. For example, if the user typed `/loop check the deploy`, pass `/loop check the deploy` as the prompt.
5. **If you were woken by a `<task-notification>`** rather than this prompt: handle the event in the context of the loop task, then call ScheduleWakeup again with the same `prompt` and the same 1200–1800s `delaySeconds` from step 4 — the Monitor remains the wake signal; this only resets the safety net.
6. **To stop the loop**, omit the ScheduleWakeup call and TaskStop any Monitor you armed (use TaskList to find the task ID if it is no longer in context).

```text
message_type: request_bugfix
work_id: lead-ijr
description: "Tighten the existing missing-required-arg failure on `shop-templates\
  \ bootstrap` when invoked without `--shop-name`. Today (verified by Architect 2026-05-13\
  \ via probe at /tmp/probe-lead-1uo: `shop-templates bootstrap --shop-type bc --target\
  \ /tmp/probe-lead-1uo`), the call raises `TypeError: replace() argument 2 must be\
  \ str, not None` from `cli.py` line 167 inside `_render_claude_md`, because `--shop-name`\
  \ has `default=None` and no argparse-required constraint upstream; the None propagates\
  \ into `primer.replace(\"{{SHOP_NAME}}\", shop_name)`. The behavior of rejecting\
  \ the omission already exists (the program exits non-zero); what is unpinned is\
  \ the SHAPE of the rejection. Scope (additive): a single new scenario (attached)\
  \ pins the desired clean-error shape \u2014 exit 2, stderr names `--shop-name` as\
  \ a missing required argument, stderr contains no Python traceback and no `TypeError`\
  \ substring, and no scaffold artifacts (`.claude/agents/`, `.beads/`, top-level\
  \ `CLAUDE.md`, top-level `.gitignore`) are written to the target. Realization pinned\
  \ by user-driver: argparse-required for `--shop-name` (standard CLI convention;\
  \ default-derive was rejected as too surprising). Prior contracts unaffected: scenarios\
  \ for shop-type validation (d6e935bca62d0039, 7941cdc591cd2c3b) and bd-init side-effects\
  \ (0c6f1c5d9bc4226e, 32d99f6d4a2dad37) continue to hold; this only adds a new pin\
  \ on missing-shop-name diagnostic shape. Note: an in-house pattern in `_validate_shop_type`\
  \ returns 2 with a custom diagnostic shape rather than letting argparse short-circuit\
  \ (see cli.py lines 394-406 comments). The Implementer may choose to match that\
  \ shape (custom validation returning 2) or to use argparse's native `required=True`\
  \ (which also exits 2 by convention). The scenario pins exit 2 and stderr content;\
  \ the mechanism is the Implementer's call. Parent lead bead: lead-1uo."
scenarios:
- hash: 3c8612d20608e9a3
  tags:
  - '@scenario_hash:3c8612d20608e9a3'
  - '@bc:shopsystem-templates'
  gherkin: "Feature: shop-templates bootstrap rejects missing --shop-name with argparse\
    \ error\n\n  @scenario_hash:3c8612d20608e9a3 @bc:shopsystem-templates\n  Scenario:\
```
